segments.s495-g73: اگر introduces a conditional clause (‘if’). شاه is the subject (‘the king’). بیند is 3rd-person present (classical/poetic form of دیدن) used here in a conditional sense: ‘sees’. که begins the subordinate clause that follows. با + موبدان is a prepositional phrase (‘with the mōbeds’); موبدان is the plural/collective of موبد (Zoroastrian priests).
segments.s495-g74: شود is the 3rd-person present classical form of شدن here meaning ‘become / come to be’ or more idiomatically ‘will be / will go’ in narrative/optative use. نزد طینوش places the action at/near a place or person named طینوش (a proper name). با بخردان is another accompaniment phrase (‘with the wise ones’); بخردان is plural of بخرد (the wise). The whole segment states that he will be/come to Tinush accompanied by the wise.
segments.s495-g75: چو here means ‘when’ (temporal). بیندش combines بیند + the object suffix -ش (‘he sees him’). بپذیرد is the 3rd-person present/subjunctive of پذیرفتن used as a prophetic/future action ‘he will accept’. این خواسته is the direct object phrase ‘this request/wish’. Word order is typical poetic: temporal + verb + object.
segments.s495-g76: ز is the poetic preposition ‘from’/‘with’ (here ‘with/in the form of’). هرگونه‌ای means ‘of every kind’ or ‘in every manner’. گنج is ‘treasure’ and آراسته is a past-participial adjective ‘adorned’ or ‘furnished’. The phrase says the treasury will be made splendid/furnished in every way.
segments.s495-g77: بیاید here is an optative/epistolary form meaning ‘may come’ or ‘it would be fitting that he comes’. چو بیند ترا بی‌سپاه = ‘when he sees you unescorted’: چو = ‘when’, بیند = ‘he sees’, ترا is the object form ‘you’ (ت + را), and بی‌سپاه is a predicate/adjective ‘without an army / unescorted’ describing you. The clause order is poetic (verb first).
segments.s495-g78: اگر introduces a second conditional ‘if’. بازگردد is 3rd-person present/subjunctive of بازگشتن ‘to return’ (‘if he returns’). گشادست is a fused adjective/copula meaning ‘is wide/open’ (گشاد + است). راه is the noun ‘road/way’; the order here (predicate before noun) is a common poetic inversion: ‘the way is open’.
segments.s495-g79: چو = ‘when’. او is the 3rd-person pronoun ‘he’. بشنود is 3rd-person present/subjunctive of شنیدن ‘he hears’ (classical form). خوب‌گفتار is a compound adjective/noun meaning ‘good-speaking’ → ‘eloquent words’ or ‘courteous speech’. من is the possessor ‘my’ attached to خوب‌گفتار (my eloquent words / my good speech).
segments.s495-g80: نه negates the verb اندیشد (‘he does not think / does not consider’). از here means ‘about/concerning’ (introducing the topics he will not consider). رنگ in this context means ‘appearance/complexion/status’ rather than literal colour. و connects two items; بازار here refers to one’s market/trade or social standing (poetically ‘my market/origin’). من is the possessor ‘my’ attached to both nouns. The whole clause: ‘he will not think about my looks or my trade/origins.’
segments.s495-g17: بفرمود is a past honorific/polite verb meaning ‘he commanded/said’. The following clause lacks an explicit conjunction (که) — a common poetic omission — so کو را به بیرون برند is the subordinate clause of that command; برند is the present-subjunctive/jussive plural (‘they should carry/take’). را marks the definite direct object; به بیرون is a directional phrase ‘out/away’.
segments.s495-g18: ز پیش here is a prepositional phrase ‘from before/in front of’ with نشستش functioning as a noun ‘his sitting/seat/session’ (possession marked by -ش). به هامون برند continues the verbal idea: برند (subjunctive/plural) ‘they should carry’ toward هامون ‘the plain’. Word order is poetic but maps to ‘they carry [him] from before his seat to the plain.’
segments.s495-g19: چنین گفت پس is a formulaic start: چنین ‘thus/so’ + گفت (past ‘he said’) + پس ‘then/afterwards’. با سکندر به راز is a prepositional phrase ‘with Alexander, in secret’ — به راز (‘in secret’) is adverbial, indicating manner.
segments.s495-g20: که introduces the reported content (‘that…’). طینوش is a proper name; بی‌دانش is an adjectival compound ‘without knowledge’ = ‘ignorant’; دیوساز is a compound noun ‘demon‑maker’/sorcerer used predicatively. Word order is Subject (name) + adjective + predicate noun.
segments.s495-g21: نباید is an impersonal negative modal ‘it must not / one should not’. که introduces the subordinate clause. اندر نهان is an adverbial phrase ‘in secret/in hiding’ (اندر = in, نهان = hidden). چاره‌ای is an indefinite object ‘a remedy/expedient’.
segments.s495-g22: بسازد is present-subjunctive/optative (from ساختن) ‘should make/devise’ used here in the negative sense with نباید (‘should not devise’). گزندی و پتیاره‌ای are the objects: گزندی ‘harm’ and پتیاره‌ای a poetic/rare word for a harmful device/miscellaneous mischief; و simply links them.
segments.s495-g23: تو (you) is the vocative/subject. دانش‌پژوهی is a noun phrase ‘seeker/student of knowledge’ (characterizing the addressee). و داری خرد links with و ‘and’ + داری (present indicative ‘you have/possess’) + خرد ‘wisdom’, so ‘you possess wisdom.’
segments.s495-g24: نگه کن is an imperative ‘keep watch / observe’ (نگه = watch/keeping, کن = do!). بدین is an adverbial/archaic construction meaning ‘with this / regarding this’ (here written as ب + دین in the segmentation). تا چه اندر خورد is a purpose/result clause ‘until/to see what happens’; خورد here means ‘befalls/happens.’
segments.s495-g97: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. قیدافه گفت is a subordinate clause 'Qeydafeh spoke' (گفت is the past of گفتن 'to say'). The main clause follows immediately: سکندر شنید 'Alexander heard' (simple past). The word order places the subordinate clause before the main clause, a common poetic sequence.
segments.s495-g98: به چشم و دلش is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'in/into his eye and heart' (به + nouns, with the possessive suffix ش attached to دل). چاره او is the direct object 'a remedy for him' (او = 'him/her' attached after چاره). بدید is an older/past form of دیدن 'to see' meaning 'he saw' and takes چاره او as its object; Persian commonly places the object before the verb here.
segments.s495-g99: بخندید is past 'he laughed' (subject is contextually Alexander). زان چاره is 'at/from that remedy/plan' (زان = 'from that/at that') and specifies what provoked the laugh. در زیر لب is an idiomatic locution 'under the lip' = 'under his breath' (an adverbial phrase describing how he laughed).
segments.s495-g100: نهان کرد is a compound where نهان (hidden/secretly) modifies the verb کرد 'did/made', together 'hid (secretly)'. The phrase زیر قصب 'under a reed' gives location. The preceding بسد is an archaic/poetic form (here functioning with an elided object) — in context it means he closed/kept (it) or otherwise secured it before hiding; the explicit object is omitted and recovered from the prior clause (the remedy/plan).
segments.s495-g101: سکندر بیامد is the main clause 'Alexander came' (بیامد = past 'came'). ز نزدیک اوی is a prepositional/relational phrase: ز here is the poetic 'from/near', نزدیک 'near', and اوی is the independent/enclitic pronoun 'him'/'to him' — together 'came near him' or 'came up to him'. Word order places the verb before the prepositional phrase.
segments.s495-g102: پراندیشه is a compound adjective 'full of thought / pensive' or 'careworn'. بد جان is a descriptive phrase where بد qualifies جان ('ill' or 'distressed in soul'), and تاریک اوی adds 'dark (was) his' with اوی as the possessive 'his'. Together these words describe his inner state: 'pensive, ill of soul, his soul dark.' The adjectives precede the noun جان and the possessive follows it, as is typical in older Persian.
segments.s495-g9: ابرایهٔ شرط با اگر آغاز شده: 'اگر' introduces a conditional. 'نیستی' here functions as the negative copula/verb ('is not' / 'is lacking') referring to the following noun phrase; word order is inverted (verb before its logical subject), a frequent poetic device. 'فر این نامدار' is a genitival noun phrase 'the farr of this renowned one' (فر = the royal/divine glory belonging to the named person), with 'این نامدار' post‑modifying 'فر'.
segments.s495-g10: 'سرت' is the locative/possessive form 'your head' (here the thing affected) placed before the verb for emphasis. 'کندمی' is a poetic adverbial/verb form indicating slowness or tardy manner ('slowly' / 'bit by bit') describing how the action will be done. 'چون' introduces a simile 'like/as'; 'ترنجی' is the simile-term 'a roundel/medallion' (image of an ornament that detaches); 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from'; 'بار' is literal 'load/burden' (here the shoulders/body as the thing from which the head will be separated). Together they form a simile comparing removing the head to a medallion coming off a burden.
segments.s495-g11: 'هم‌اکنون' sets the temporal frame 'right now'. 'سرت را' is the definite object ('your head') marked by 'را' and fronted for emphasis. 'من' is the agent 'I' (explicit here for emphasis). 'از درد' gives the cause or circumstance 'from/because of the pain'. 'فور' (an emphatic adverb) reinforces immediacy ('at once / suddenly'). The clause therefore prepares the object and circumstance before the verb in the next segment.
segments.s495-g12: 'به لشکر نمایم' contains the verb 'نمایم' (first person singular present/subjunctive form used narratively to express an immediate action—here effectively 'I will show') with 'به لشکر' marking the indirect object/direction 'to the army'. The following participial phrase 'ز تن کرده دور' uses 'ز' = 'from', 'تن' = 'the body', and the verbal adjective/participle 'کرده' ('having made / having done'), plus 'دور' ('removed, away') to mean 'having removed (it) from the body' — a past participial circumstantial clause describing how the head will be shown.
segments.s495-g13: 'یکی' is an indefinite subject 'someone / a certain one'. 'بانگ برزد' is an idiom meaning 'a cry rose / a cry was uttered' — 'برزد' is an archaic poetic verb used with 'بانگ' to mean 'went up' or 'was struck forth'. 'برو' is the imperative 'go!' and attaches as direct speech; 'مادرش' is the vocative/indirect object 'to his mother' with the enclitic 'ش' = 'his'. The segment reports that somebody shouted the command addressed to the mother.
segments.s495-g14: The relative/resultive connector 'که' links to what happened next: 'آسیمه برگشت' is a verbl phrase 'returned hastily/distraught' (آسیمه = headlong/hasty), with the implied subject being the mother from the previous segment. 'جنگی سرش' is a genitival phrase 'the warrior's head' (جنگی = warlike/fighter; سر = head; ش = 'his'), specifying what occasioned her panic. Word order places the predicate 'returned' before the possessed noun phrase which explains why she returned.
segments.s495-g15: 'به طینوش گفت' is a ditransitive reporting clause 'he said to Tīnūsh' (به marks the indirect object; طینوش is a proper name). In 'این نه گفتار اوست' 'این' = 'this', 'نه' negates the following noun, so 'not this'; 'گفتار' = 'speech/utterance' and 'اوست' is the copula 'it is his' (literally 'this is not his speech'). Note that 'گفت' here functions as the past-tense verb 'said' in narrative (the senses listed are nominal but the function is verbal here).
segments.s495-g16: 'بران' ('upon that' / 'thereupon') links the next action; 'درگه' is 'the court' (royal court). 'او را فرستاد' is 'he sent him/it' (او = he; را marks the direct object), with 'فرستاد' as the past-tense verb 'sent'. 'دوست' is the object of sending 'a friend' (he sent a friend to that court). The clause therefore narrates a consequential action: upon saying that, he dispatched a friend to the court.
segments.s495-g65: This clause is a compact classical Persian noun-phrase plus verb: particle + place + numeral/indefinite + noun + verb + locative. "به جایی" = "at/to a place/spot" (prepositional phrase), "یکی بیشه" = "a certain grove/thicket" with "یکی" as an indefinite/partitive, and "دیدم" is perfect/past "I saw." The final "به راه" is a locative phrase "by/at the road," placed after the verb for poetic word order.
segments.s495-g66: Parse: "نشانم ترا در کمین با سپاه" — "نشان" here means ‘mark/ sign’ and takes enclitic person markers; the sequence expresses that the speaker has taken note of or found the addressee as being situated. The cluster "-م" and "-ت" are enclitic person markers (first/second) attached in the verse; "ترا/را" functions as the object marker for "you." "در کمین با سپاه" is a prepositional phrase: "in ambush with an army." Note the classical tendency to attach pronouns and object markers as enclitics.
segments.s495-g67: "شوم" is an optative/jussive form (subjunctive) meaning "may I become / would that I be" (classical Persian use of the present-subjunctive to express a wish). "من" is the emphatic subject "I." "ز پیش تو" = "from before you / leaving your presence," and "در پیش اوی" = "into his presence" ("اوی" is an archaic/poetic form of the third-person pronoun meaning "him/ his"). Word order reflects poetic inversion: wish + subject + departure phrase + arrival phrase.
segments.s495-g68: "ببینم" is subjunctive/optative "may I see / let me see." The noun phrase "روان بداندیش اوی" = "his ill‑minded soul/spirit," where "روان" = "soul/spirit," "بداندیش" = adjective "ill‑minded, malicious," and "اوی" is the possessive/third‑person pronoun "his." In classical Persian the possessor often follows the adjective and is written as the enclitic pronoun.
segments.s495-g69: "بگویم که" uses the verb in the subjunctive/optative sense "let me say / I would say that" — a common rhetorical opening. "چندین فرستاد چیز" follows classical (somewhat inverted) word order: "چندین" (several/many) modifies the verb "فرستاد" (sent/dispatched), with the theme "چیز" (thing/matter) following; literally "he sent several things."
segments.s495-g70: "کزان" = "from that / from which" linking back to the previous clause; "پس" = "afterwards/then;" "نیندیشی" is a negative imperative/jussive (2nd person) meaning "do not think" (classical negative present-subjunctive form). "از چیز نیز" = "about any thing as well" ("از" = about/of; "نیز" = also/even). The whole gives a prohibition: "after that, do not think on any other thing either."
segments.s495-g71: "فرستاده گوید که" = "the messenger says that…"; "گوید" is the classical present/habitual form of "to say" (reporting clause). "من نزد شاه" = "I (in) the presence of the king" where "نزد" means "in the presence of / before (someone)." This begins a reported content clause introduced by "که."
segments.s495-g72: "نیارم شدن" here is an archaic/poetic negative construction indicating that the speaker will not be made/allowed or will not come to a state; read as passive/potential sense with "شدن" (to become / to be made). "در میان سپاه" = "in the midst of the army." Together the phrase yields: "I will not be (brought/allowed) to be before the king in the middle of the army." Note: the exact lexical history of "نیارم" is irregular/archaic in this verse and functions as a negative modal form.
segments.s495-g1: چو is a temporal conjunction here meaning “when.” طینوش is a proper name (the speaker). گفت and شنید are simple past verbs (third person): گفت = “said” and شنید = “heard.” The clause order is chronological: “When Tinush said (something), Alexander heard.”
segments.s495-g2: به کردار باد is an adverbial phrase: به + کردار “in the manner/behavior” and باد “the wind,” so “in the manner of the wind” (i.e. swiftly). دمان “hem/skirt” is the object and بردمید is a past transitive verb meaning “carried off / seized.” The subject is implied from the previous clause (سکندر).
segments.s495-g3: بدو is the prepositional phrase ‘to him’ (به + او), and گفت is the past ‘said.’ کای is a vocative particle “O,” followed by two coordinated insults ناکس بی‌خرد “wretch, senseless.” Word order is indirect speech marker + verb + vocative phrase.
segments.s495-g4: ترا را is the object ‘you’ with the object marker را attached (here split into ت + را). مردم is the subject “the people,” از مردمان “from men,” and نشمرد is the (negative) form of شمردن “to count/ reckon.” Together: “the people do not count you among men.”
segments.s495-g5: ندانی is the present-negative second-person verb “you do not know.” The doubled که (که پیش که) introduces an embedded relative/interrogative: پیش = “in the presence of / before,” and the second که introduces the person ‘whom.’ داری here functions as an auxiliary (“you have / you are”) with نشست “seat/sitting,” so the phrase means “you do not know before whom you have sat/taken a seat.”
segments.s495-g6: بر شاه منشین is an imperative negative: منشین “do not sit (down) before the king” (بر = “before/on [the king]”). و links the clauses and منمای دست is another negative imperative: منمای “do not show/reveal” + دست “hand,” here idiomatically meaning power or force — “do not display your hand (power).”
segments.s495-g7: سرت پر ز ... است is a copular descriptive clause: سرت “your head” + پر ز “full of” + تیزی “sharpness/keenness” و کنداوریست—the compound ends with -است meaning “is” (here condensed as کنداوری-ست). کنداوری refers to digging/treasure-seeking, so the whole clause says “your head is full of sharpness and of digging/treasure-seeking.”
segments.s495-g8: نگویی is a negative imperative/hortative “do not say” (do not tell). مرا (here split into م + را) is the object “to me” (را is the object marker), and خود “yourself” emphasizes the agent—“do not yourself tell me.” The rest is an embedded interrogative clause: که شاه تو کیست “who your king is,” with کیست the copula-interrogative “is who.” Overall: “Do not yourself tell me who your king is.”
segments.s495-g57: سکندر (proper name) is the subject. بیامد is simple past 'he came'. ز جای is a prepositional phrase literally 'from the place' used here idiomatically with نشست ('sat') to mean 'sat down' or 'took a seat' — نشســت is past 'he sat'. Word order is subject + past verb(s); the sequence gives two actions: he came and then sat.
segments.s495-g58: برین is the fused form بر + این ('upon this' / 'on this') — an archaic/poetic spelling. عهد here means 'oath/pledge'. بگرفت is past 'he took' or 'he seized' and governs the object phrase دستش به دست, literally 'his hand to (another's) hand' (i.e. they clasped hands). ش is the enclitic possessive 'his'.
segments.s495-g59: بپرسید is the narrative past 'they asked' (archaic short past form). طینوش is a proper name here. کاین = 'this' (demonstrative). چون ... کنی is an interrogative clause 'how (چون) you do (کنی)' — کنی is the present/subjunctive form used in indirect question; together it asks 'how do you do this?'.
segments.s495-g60: بدین is the fused/prepositional form (به این) 'by/with this' (archaic spelling). جادوی here names the magical art ('magic, sorcery') and is governed by بدین. بر چه = 'upon/for what' (بر as preposition + چه 'what/for what'); افسون کنی is a verb phrase 'you enchant / you cast a charm' where کنی is subjunctive/present used in the indirect question. The whole asks 'By this magic, for what (purpose) do you enchant?'
segments.s495-g61: بدو گفت is the ditransitive sequence 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him', گفت = 'said'); in poetic narrative the short form is used. چون introduces a temporal/subordinate clause 'when/as': چون بازگردم ز شاه = 'when I return from the king'. بازگردم is first-person singular present/subjunctive ('I return' or 'I will return' in context).
segments.s495-g62: تو باید که ... is a modal construction: تو 'you' + باید 'must/ought' + که introducing the subordinate clause. با من بیایی is 'come with me' where بیایی is the subjunctive/optative second person 'you come' used after باید ('you must come'). به راه 'on the road' / 'on the journey' indicates accompaniment direction/purpose.
segments.s495-g63: ز لشکر = 'from the army' (ز = from). بیاری here is the archaic fused prepositional sense 'به یاری' meaning 'to the aid/with the help of' (rendered as 'to my aid/for aid'); it modifies the following noun phrase. سواری هزار = 'a thousand horsemen' (poetic ordering places the numeral after the noun). سواری is the noun 'rider/horseman'.
segments.s495-g64: همه نامدار = 'all renowned' (همه 'all/everyone', نامدار 'famous, illustrious'). از در کارزار is a genitival/ablative phrase 'from/of the field of battle' (از + در + کارزار) giving the domain in which they are famed: 'renowned on the battlefield.' Word order is compact and poetic: quantity and epithet follow the moving noun phrase.
segments.s495-g49: گرین را که گفتی به جای آوری — This clause is a relative/imperative construction: گرین را (object + rā) marks 'Garin' as the direct object of آوری (to bring). که گفتی ('which/whom you said') is a relative clause qualifying گرین; که = 'that/whom' precedes the verb گفتی ('you said') as in classical word order. به جای آوری literally 'bring to (his) place' with به جای (to place) + آوری (2nd/imperative sense 'bring').
segments.s495-g50: بکوشی و پاکیزه رای آوری — بکوشی is an exhortation/imperative-subjunctive 'strive / try' (2nd‑person); و links the clauses 'and'. پاکیزه modifies رای: a poetic adjective 'pure/clean' qualifying رای ('opinion/counsel'), and آوری is repeated 'bring' (i.e. 'bring/produce that pure counsel'). The verb forms are hortative/optative (you should/strive to...).
segments.s495-g51: من از گنج وز بدره و هرچ هست — من 'I' is the subject. از introduces a list of possessions/items 'from/of': گنج 'treasure', (وز بدره) here is a poetic coordination: وز is a fused/archaic connective (see note) linking بدره 'full moon / beauty' and هرچ هست 'and whatever exists'. هست is the copula 'is/exists'; the phrase means 'treasures, moonlike beauties, and whatever else there is.'
segments.s495-g52: ز اسپان و مردان خسرو پرست — ز (poetic variant of از) again 'from/of' introduces another group: اسپان 'horses' and مردان 'men' (here used as heroic/animate attendants). خسرو پرست is a compound: خسرو (Khosrow, here a sovereign) + پرست (suffix '‑worshipper'/'worshipper of'), so 'men who worship Khosrow' or 'Khosrow‑worshipping men.'
segments.s495-g53: ترا بخشم و نیز دارم سپاس — ترا (written here split as ت + را) is the object 'you' (t + rā = 'you' with object marker). بخشم is a present/future hortatory or indicative 'I forgive' / 'I will forgive' or 'I grant forgiveness'. و نیز 'and also' connects the next clause; دارم سپاس literally 'I have thanks' = 'I am grateful' or 'I give thanks.' Together: 'I forgive you, and moreover I am thankful.'
segments.s495-g54: تو باشی جهانگیر و نیکی‌شناس — باشی here is an optative/imperative-subjunctive 'may you be' / 'be thou' addressing 'تو' (you). جهانگیر is an epithet 'world‑conquering' (or a name/title) and نیکی‌شناس 'one who recognizes / appreciates good' (i.e. a good‑judge or grateful person). The structure is a wish: 'May you be world‑conquering and appreciative of good.'
segments.s495-g55: یکی پاک دستور باشی مرا — یکی 'one/a single' or 'a certain' modifies the following predicate; پاک دستور 'pure commander/order' is a nominal phrase where پاک (pure) qualifies دستور (command/order/leader). باشی is the 2nd‑person optative/imperative 'be', and مرا (here written split as م + را) is the object 'to me' ('me' + object marker). The clause means 'Be to me a single/pure commander.'
segments.s495-g56: بدین مرز گنجور باشی مرا — بدین is a fused poetic form (به + این) 'to/for this'; مرز 'border/frontier' is the location; گنجور 'treasure‑laden' or 'treasury' qualifies the desired state; باشی again 'be (you)'. مرا (split as م + را) repeats 'for me'. The sense: 'Be (for) this frontier a treasure‑house for me.' Note the poetic, clipped morphology (به+این → بدین; مرا split) common in Ferdowsi.
segments.s495-g89: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause. طینوش is a proper name (the subject) and بشنید is the simple past 3rd‑person 'he heard'. زان ('from that/that') refers back to the preceding news, and شاد شد is an inchoative construction (adjective + شد) meaning 'became glad'.
segments.s495-g90: بسان is a simile particle 'like/as'. یکی is an indefinite numeral 'a single', modifying سرو 'cypress' (a common Persian epithet for a tall, proud person), and آزاد is a predicate adjective 'free/proud'; شد again is the inchoative past 'became'. The whole phrase is a poetic simile describing his posture or mood after rejoicing.
segments.s495-g91: چنین 'thus/so' modifies the reporting verb. داد is the past 3rd‑person 'gave' and پاسخ is its object 'answer' — the formula 'چنین داد پاسخ' = 'he answered thus'. The conjunction که introduces the content clause, and دارم امید is a present tense first‑person expression 'I have hope'.
segments.s495-g92: که introduces a subordinate result/wish clause. گردد is the subjunctive/potential of 'become' (here expressing 'may/ will become'), بدو is the dative pronoun 'for/to him', تیره modifies روزم ('my day/fate') giving 'my dark/gloomy day' (روزم = روز + possessive -م), and سپید is the predicate adjective 'white/bright' — the poetic image of gloom turning bright. Note the verb precedes the nominal phrase for poetic inversion.
segments.s495-g93: به دام من is a prepositional phrase 'into/to my trap/snare' (به + noun + possessive pronoun). آویزد is a 3rd‑person verb meaning 'clings/is caught' (here used narratively as 'was caught' or 'fell into'), او is the subject 'he', and ناگهان is the adverb 'suddenly' placed for emphasis at the end.
segments.s495-g94: به here functions instrumentally 'by/with' introducing the cause or means. خونی (literally 'of blood'/'bloody') is the noun/phrase referring to the blood, که introduces a relative clause 'that', او ریخت is the past 'he shed/poured', and اندر جهان 'in the world' locates the action; together 'by the blood that he shed in the world'.
segments.s495-g95: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. دارای is a participial/attributive form 'possessing' or 'wealthy' modifying دارا (here either a title or name 'Darā'); و links the next noun, and گردان سند is a nominal phrase 'the army/host (گردان) of Sind (سند)'. The segment lists actors in a temporal clause and relies on poetic compactness/apposition.
segments.s495-g96: This clause is parallel and again begins with چو 'when'. فور here is ambiguous in isolation (it can mean 'immediately' or be a proper/name in epic contexts); دلیر is 'brave', آن is the demonstrative 'that/the', سرافراز is an epithet 'lofty/proud', and هند is 'India/the Indians'. The phrase characterizes another prominent warrior of India; word order is condensed and typical of epic diction.
segments.s495-g33: مرا is the object pronoun 'me' written as م + را (را is the object/definite marker attached). نژندی means 'disgrace/insult', and ز + اسکندرست = 'from Alexander is' (اسکندرست is a contracted poetic form of اسکندر است, ‘Alexander is / it is Alexander’). The whole clause states the source of the speaker's humiliation: 'This disgrace of mine is from Alexander.'
segments.s495-g34: کجا here is a rhetorical 'how/where' introducing incredulity. شاد = 'happy/joyful'. با ... با is a simple coordination 'with ... and with ...'. افسرست is poetic/contracted (افسر است) 'is a helmet' or 'is the helmet'. The clause questions how Alexander could be joyful while wearing crown and helmet.
segments.s495-g35: بدین سان = 'in this way/thus' (ب is the prepositional/prefix element, دین+سان together form the adverbial phrase). فرستد is the narrative/past third-person 'sent' used in epic style. مرا نزد شاه = 'me to the presence of the king' (مرا object, نزد = 'near/in the presence of'). The clause means 'Thus he sent me to the king.'
segments.s495-g36: که introduces purpose/result here ('that / so that'). از نامور مهتری = 'from/of a famed preeminent one' (نامور 'renowned', مهتری as 'chief/elder/preeminent person'). باژ خواه = 'will/would request/seek the cry' (باژ = 'cry/rallying cry', خواه = verb 'to want/ask' in classical usage). The whole clause expresses the mission: to obtain a famed leader's rallying-cry.
segments.s495-g37: بدان is an imperative 'know this' formed from ب + دان (ب as prefix and دان 'know'). تا هرآن بد که خواهد رسید = 'whenever any evil that will come' — تا + هرآن gives the sense 'whenever/every time that', بد = 'bad/evil', که introduces the subordinate clause, خواهد رسید is future/auxiliary + verb 'will reach/arrive'. The clause is a general warning: 'Know that whenever any misfortune will arrive...'
segments.s495-g38: آید پدید is a verbal cluster meaning 'will come forth/appear' (آید 'comes', پدید 'appear, arise'). بر من آید = 'will come upon me' (بر = 'upon', من = 'me'). ز دشمن = 'from the enemy'. برو here is an archaic/interjectional particle that precedes the clause (in narrative often an intensifier or connective); the clause states the outcome: the misfortune will appear from the enemy and fall upon me.
segments.s495-g39: ورا is an object/indirect-object pronoun 'to him/unto that' (referring back to the addressee). من is emphatic subject 'I'. بدین زود = 'in this short time/quickly' (ب + دین as before; زود = 'soon/quickly'). پاسخ دهم is first-person singular present-subjunctive used for near future 'I will give an answer / I will reply'. The clause: 'I will quickly answer him.'
segments.s495-g40: یکی = 'one/a certain' (here 'one' = 'a single' or 'a certain [counsel]'). شاه را = 'to the king' (را marks the definite object/recipient). رای فرخ = 'an auspicious/fortunate counsel' (رای = 'counsel/plan', فرخ = 'fortunate/auspicious'). نهم (not listed among words) is the first-person present-subjunctive of 'نهادن'/'نَمُدَن' meaning 'I put/give'—here 'I will give the king one good counsel.'
segments.s495-g25: سکندر (proper name) + بدو (indirect object 'to him') + گفت (past tense 'said') is the reporting clause. کین نیست راست is a small predicate clause meaning 'this hatred is not right': کین (hatred/vengeance) occurs before the copula نیست (is not), and راست ('right/true') is a predicate adjective following the negated copula.
segments.s495-g26: چو here is a subordinating conjunction ('when/for/since') introducing the reason clause. طینوش را uses را as the definite object marker, so طینوش is the object. بازخوانی functions as a verbal noun/infinitival action ('calling back' / 're-calling' / 're-reading') that serves as the subject of رواست (روا + است), i.e. 'it is fitting/permissible.' ست is the contracted copula 'is.'
segments.s495-g27: جهاندار is the subject ('the world-ruler/sovereign'). فرزند را is the definite object (را marks 'the son'). بازخواند is a past transitive verb 'called back / summoned / recited again' describing the action the sovereign took. Word order is Subject–Object–Verb, typical of Persian.
segments.s495-g28: بران = 'upon that / thereon' (an adverbial/locative), نامور is an adjective/noun 'the famed/renowned one' modifying the person, زیرگاهش ('زیرگاه' + possessive -ش) 'his low place/under-seat' gives the location, and نشاند is the past causative/ transitive verb '(he) set/caused to sit' (archaic past form often used for 'caused to sit' or 'seated'). The clause describes placing the famed person in a low seat.
segments.s495-g29: Another reporting clause: سکندر (subject) + بدو (to him) + گفت (said). کای is the vocative particle 'O' addressing someone directly, so کای کامگار is 'O, successful one' (کامگار is an address meaning 'one who attains desire').
segments.s495-g30: اگر introduces a conditional 'if.' کام دل is a genitival phrase 'the desire of the heart' (کام + دل). خواهی is second-person present ('you want' / 'you wish') giving the protasis 'if you want.' آرام دار is an imperative-like instructive: آرام (calm) + دار (here functioning as a verb 'keep/hold' in an older/poetic usage), so together 'keep calm' or 'be at peace.'
segments.s495-g31: من (I) is the subject. از تو ('from you' / 'against you') marks the target/agent. بدین is an archaic compound (ب + دین) meaning 'with this / for this' and introduces the object of the negated verb. کین (hatred/vengeance) is the noun being refused. نگیرم is the first-person present/subjunctive/negative form of گرفتن 'to take' used here idiomatically 'I will not take (revenge)' and همی is an archaic particle adding emphasis/continuity ('ever/indeed').
segments.s495-g32: سخن (speech/words) is the object/topic. هرچ is a relative/indefinite 'whatever' and گویی is the present/subjunctive form 'you may say' or 'you say'—together 'whatever you say.' پذیرم is first-person present 'I accept' (پذیرفتن), and the clause-ending همی is an emphatic/archaic particle, reinforcing the speaker's readiness to accept.
segments.s495-g81: بیاید is a 3rd-person present/subjunctive/optative form often used in classical Persian narrative to mean 'he comes' or 'may he come'; the verb appears first (poetic inversion). بر is a preposition here meaning 'toward/to' followed by the demonstrative آن 'that' which directly precedes the head noun سایه 'shade'. The phrase زیر درخت is a locative modifier 'under the tree' that follows سایه, so the full phrase reads 'to/into that shade beneath the tree.'
segments.s495-g82: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; گنجور here is used as a noun 'treasury/treasury-house' (not an adjective). می‌خواهد is a 3rd-person present 'wants/asks for' and governs the following coordinated objects, linked by و. تاج و تخت is a simple coordination 'the crown and the throne' (و repeated for poetic weight).
segments.s495-g83: تو is the 2nd-person subject 'you' (poetic/thou). جنگی modifies سپاهی (an adjectival/attributive sequence: 'warlike army' or 'warrior soldiery'). به گردش is a prepositional phrase 'into/for a circuit/campaign', and درآر is an imperative/2nd-person verb 'put/bring (into motion)' (poetic present-imperative form of درآوردن). The clause literally orders: 'You, make (put) a warlike army into motion.'
segments.s495-g84: برآساید is a 3rd-person optative/subjunctive meaning 'may he be eased' or 'he will find ease' — a result/state clause referring back to the effected person. از marks source ('from/out of'); گردش روزگار is a fixed collocation 'the turning/course of fortune' (گردش = turn/course; روزگار = fate/time). The clause therefore reads 'he will be relieved from the turns of fate.'
segments.s495-g85: مکافات is a nominal subject meaning 'recompense/reward' (here offered by the speaker). من follows it as a postnominal possessive 'my' (classical order). باشد is a 3rd-person subjunctive/jussive 'let it be' or 'may it be,' used idiomatically to grant or resign something. و introduces the next nominal phrase کام تو 'your desire' (کام = desire; تو = you/your).
segments.s495-g86: نجوید is a 3rd-person negative subjunctive/jussive 'may he/they not seek' (classical poetic use to express 'let no one seek'). ازان پس is a temporal phrase 'after that' (ازان = 'from that'; پس = 'after/then'). کس in this negative context functions as 'anyone/no one' (indefinite negative sense). آرام تو places the noun آرام 'peace/rest' before the possessive pronoun تو 'your' (classical word order: noun + enclitic pronoun), so the whole clause means 'may no one thereafter seek your desire/peace.'
segments.s495-g87: که is a subordinating/relative particle 'that/who' introducing a clause describing what will happen. آید is 3rd-person present 'comes'; به دستت is a locative phrase 'into your hand/power' where the 2nd-person suffix ت attached to دست marks possession ('your hand'). بسی is an intensifier 'many/much' (classical usage) modifying خواسته 'requests/wishes' (here a plural/collective noun 'petitioners' or 'many asks'). The phrase reads 'that many requests/petitioners will come into your hand.'
segments.s495-g88: پرستنده is a noun 'worshipper/devotee/petitioner' (here part of a list of things that will accompany the arrivals). The conjunction و links it to اسپ 'steed/horse', which is then qualified by the postposed adjective آراسته 'adorned' (Persian commonly places adjectives after the noun in older/poetic language). So the sequence lists 'worshippers and adorned horses/steeds' alongside the earlier 'many petitioners.'
segments.s495-g41: اگر introduces a conditional clause ('if'). The phrase places the object before the subject and verb for emphasis: دست او (his hand) + من (I) + بگیرم (I take). بگیرم is a 1st-person present/subjunctive form used in conditional contexts. The final به دست is an instrumental/locative phrase ('by/into the hand') forming the idiom 'take by the hand.'
segments.s495-g42: به نزد تو is a prepositional phrase meaning 'to your presence / before you.' آرم here is an archaic or dialectal first-person form corresponding to 'I will bring / I bring' (cf. classical آورم) used after the conditional; it expresses the result of the condition. به جای نشست is a goal/result phrase: به جای = 'to the place/seat' and نشست (here) functions as 'to sit / to be seated' (resultative).
segments.s495-g43: بدان سان که is a fixed classical phrase meaning 'in such a way that / so that.' It introduces a clause describing manner. با او sets the accompaniment ('with him'), and نبینی is the second-person present/subjunctive (here negative, functioning like a future/general statement) 'you do not / will not see.' سپاه is the noun 'army' as the thing not seen.
segments.s495-g44: This is a negative correlative list introduced by نه ... نه ... ('neither ... nor ...'). شمشیر بینی repeats the verb بینی ('you see') attached to the first item; the second not-clause (تخت و کلاه) is coordinated with the first, implying 'you see neither sword nor throne and crown.' The verb بینی is the same 2nd-person form as in the previous segment.
segments.s495-g45: The opening چه بخشی tu ... is an indefinite/relative construction: چه ... بخشی = 'whatever portion / whatever you give'. Word order is somewhat inverted for emphasis (object/indefinite element before the verb or pronoun). زین پادشاهی literally 'of/from this kingship' marks the domain (this kingship). مرا ... را is the object referring to 'me' (the beneficiary).
segments.s495-g46: چو here means 'if' (conditional). بپسندی is 2nd-person present/subjunctive 'you approve / you choose,' introducing the dependent conditional clause. این نیک‌خواهی مرا is a possessive/nominal phrase meaning 'this goodwill of mine / my being a well-wisher' (word order: noun + مرا for 'my ... / me as the possessor'). The clause as a whole says that, if you approve, this should be taken as goodwill.
segments.s495-g47: چو بشنید is a temporal clause 'when he heard.' بشنید is past tense 'he heard.' طینوش is the subject (a proper name). گفت is the main past-tense verb 'he said,' and این سخن is the direct object 'this speech / these words.' The clause order is typical classical narrative: temporal clause + subject + verb + object.
segments.s495-g48: شنیدم is 'I heard' (perfect/past). نباید is an impersonal modal 'it must not / should not' and introduces a subordinate clause with که. گردد is subjunctive/passive-ish 'become / turn out to be,' and کهن is an adjective 'old / ancient.' Together the clause reports: 'I have heard that it ought not to become old.'

sentences.s495-s37: If the king sees that you are with the mobeds, he will go to Tinush with the wise men.
sentences.s495-s38: When he sees him, he will accept this request and furnish the treasury with every kind [of treasure].
sentences.s495-s39: He may come when he sees you unescorted; if he returns, the road is wide/open.
sentences.s495-s40: When he hears my eloquent words, he will not concern himself with my appearance or my market/trade.
sentences.s495-s9: He ordered that they carry him out and take him from before his seat to the plain.
sentences.s495-s10: Thus he then spoke privately with Alexander: that Tinush is an ignorant maker of demons.
sentences.s495-s11: One must not, in secret, devise any remedy that causes harm or mischief.
sentences.s495-s12: You are a seeker of knowledge and possess wisdom; keep watch at this to see what will befall.
sentences.s495-s49: When Qeydafeh spoke, Alexander heard; with his eyes and heart he saw a remedy for him.
sentences.s495-s50: He laughed at that remedy under his breath; then he secretly hid (it) beneath a reed.
sentences.s495-s51: Alexander came near him; anxious and troubled, his soul was dark.
sentences.s495-s5: If the farr (divine glory) of this famed one is not (with you), I will unloose your head from your shoulders slowly, like a medallion from its load.
sentences.s495-s6: This very instant I will, because of the pain, at once show your head to the army, having removed it from the body.
sentences.s495-s7: Someone cried out, 'Go to his mother!' — she returned, distraught, (to see) the warrior's head.
sentences.s495-s8: He said to Tīnūsh, 'This is not his speech,' and then sent his friend to that court.
sentences.s495-s33: At one spot I saw a thicket by the road.
sentences.s495-s34: Would that I might leave your presence and stand before him, to behold his wicked spirit.
sentences.s495-s35: Let me say that he sent many things; from that, afterwards, do not think about any other thing either.
sentences.s495-s36: The messenger says that I shall not be brought before the king in the midst of the army.
sentences.s495-s1: When Tinush spoke, Alexander heard; like the wind he seized the hem.
sentences.s495-s2: He said to him, “O wretch, senseless one; people do not reckon you among men.”
sentences.s495-s3: You do not know before whom you have taken a seat; do not sit before the king, and do not display your hand.
sentences.s495-s4: Your head is full of sharpness and of digging/treasure-seeking; do not yourself tell me who your king is.
sentences.s495-s29: Alexander came and sat down; upon this pledge he took (someone's) hand in his.
sentences.s495-s30: They asked Tinosh, 'How do you do this? By this sorcery, upon what do you cast your spell?'
sentences.s495-s31: He said to him, 'When I return from the king, you must come with me on the road.'
sentences.s495-s32: Bring from the army, to my aid, a thousand horsemen — all renowned in the field of battle.
sentences.s495-s25: Bring back the Garin you mentioned to his place; strive, and bring forth a pure counsel.
sentences.s495-s26: I have treasures, moonlike beauties and whatever else exists, and horses and men who worship Khosrow.
sentences.s495-s27: I forgive you, and moreover I hold (you) in thanks; may you be world‑conquering and appreciative of good.
sentences.s495-s28: Be (to me) a single, pure commander; be to this frontier a treasury for me.
sentences.s495-s45: When Tinush heard that he became glad; like a single, proud cypress he was freed.
sentences.s495-s46: He answered thus: 'I have hope that my dark day will turn bright for him.'
sentences.s495-s47: Suddenly he was caught in my snare by the blood he had shed in the world.
sentences.s495-s48: When Dara the wealthy and the host of Sind, and when (also) the brave, proud one of India ...
sentences.s495-s17: This humiliation toward me comes from Alexander; how can he be joyful with crown and helmet?
sentences.s495-s18: Thus he sent me to the king, that he might demand a rallying-cry from a renowned chief.
sentences.s495-s19: Know this: whenever any evil is about to occur, it will arise and come upon me from the enemy.
sentences.s495-s20: I will answer him quickly; I will give the king one auspicious counsel.
sentences.s495-s13: Alexander said to him, “This hatred is not right, for it is fitting to call back Tinush.”
sentences.s495-s14: The sovereign summoned the son and seated that famed one on his lower seat.
sentences.s495-s15: Alexander said to him, 'O successful one, if you want your heart’s desire, keep calm.'
sentences.s495-s16: I will not take vengeance on you for this; I will accept whatever you say.
sentences.s495-s41: He comes into that shade beneath the tree, and from the treasury he asks for the crown and the throne.
sentences.s495-s42: You raise a warlike army and set it in motion; he will find ease from the turns of fortune.
sentences.s495-s43: Let that be my recompense, and may no one after that seek your desire or disturb your peace.
sentences.s495-s44: For many a petitioner will come into your hand, with worshippers and adorned steeds.
sentences.s495-s21: If I take him by the hand, I will bring him to your presence and seat him in his place.
sentences.s495-s22: In such a way that you will see no army with him — neither sword, nor throne and crown.
sentences.s495-s23: Whatever share of this kingship you grant me — if you approve it — consider me a well-wisher.
sentences.s495-s24: When Tinush heard this speech he said, 'I have heard that it must not become old.'
